Hey. It's Jeff. I've got a really cool interview for you here with Sean Platt and Johnny B.
Truant. This is the deal…the question is "Can you use Product Launch Formula for fiction?" The
answer is probably not, not the way the standard PLF. I mean, you're not going to do three
videos and a sales video for a $5, $6 novel or even a more expensive hardcover. Probably not
going to use the classic peel-off launch, but you can use much of what's within PLF in your
launch for your fiction book.
Sean and Johnny launch a lot of books. As you're going to hear, they've got a publishing
company with actually, I think, five imprints. They're publishing in a variety of niches. They
publish a little bit of nonfiction, but it's mostly fiction. Everything from science fiction, to…I
don't know, to fantasy, to horror, to all kinds of different things. They publish a lot of books.
They are nonstop publishing them.
The really cool thing about this is they're going to give you the entire business model they
use. It is very unique. I don't know anyone else that is doing it like they are doing it, and they're
doing really, really well. The great thing, the thing I love about this, and you're going to hear this
towards the end of the interview…it takes publishing and being a writer out of this realm of you
have to be lucky, and a few people are lucky to just making it about math. You're going to see
what I mean as we get into this interview.
I know you're going to love this. It's a wide-ranging interview. We talk a little bit about
nonfiction, but most of it's about fiction. There’s some incredible wisdom in this interview.
Without further ado, let's get started.
Hey, everyone. It's Jeff. I'm here which Johnny B. Truant and Sean Platt. We're going to
be talking about book launches, or books. Beyond launches, actually selling books and actually
making money. Now, I know a lot of you want to change the world with your book, and that's
awesome, but you've got to get it in a lot of people's hands. This is about making money and
selling lots of books.
Welcome, guys. I'm really excited to have you here. I've gotten to know Sean over the
last, I don't know, year or two because I'm in a Mastermind group with him, and this is actually
my first time talking to Johnny, but I feel like I know you because I've been reading both of your
work. Could you just tell us a little bit about what you guys are doing in your business?
Johnny Truant: 	Sean, you started the business ...

Sean Platt: 	All right. Cool. Our nonfiction line is a lot of fun. It's basically, on the podcast that
Johnny was talking about, The Self Publishing Podcast, it's me and Johnny and Dave. We get
together once a week. It just started out as an environment for us to talk about what we were
doing. It's like a little mastermind that we just record.
At the time, Johnny and I were not writing together. In fact, this is kind of funny,
because Johnny kind of talked us into it. Dave and I were over doing our thing. At the time, we
were serializing on Amazon. To my knowledge, we were the first people to ever use the words
"Episode and season" in regard to serialized e-book fiction. It was shorthand for the reader to
say, "Hey, you're not getting a piece of a book, it's this different experience. It's more like Lost,
but on your Kindle."
We were pumping out a book a week. Now, a book was 20,000 words at that time, but
that was every week, because what happened is we were launching these, and we realized,
"Wow, no church meets every other Sunday. We really need to be there every week because we
want to be a habit." We just…every Tuesday…we piggybacked off of that's media day, new DVD's
come out Tuesday, new music comes out Tuesday. We want to be a part of that culture.
Every Tuesday, we had a new episode out. We did that until we just couldn't do it
anymore. Basically, once Dave and I stopped doing that, Johnny and I started writing really,
really furiously. When Johnny joined the podcast, he was the everyman. He's like, "I could never
write as fast as you guys."
Johnny Truant: 	Or with another writer.
Sean Platt: 	"Or with another writer. I'm a solo writer." Well, it took him 12 years to get his
first book done. Since then, we started writing together in 2013, so just last year.
Johnny Truant: 	2012, Sean.
Sean Platt: 	Oh, 2012. In 2013, we wrote a million and a half words, which is a Harry and a
Potter series, and a half worth of copy all fiction, well, except for Write, Publish, Repeat, which
was basically us kind of distilling what we do and how we do it, because the podcast, one of the
things that people love most about it is that we're three guys talking. We’re generally off-topic
quite a lot. We do get to the point eventually, but we are off-topic a lot, and Write, Publish,
Repeat wasn't. Even though it's a little more verbose than most nonfiction, for sure, it does talk
about what we do in a more linear way than the podcast does. Since then, Johnny and I write a
couple million words a year. We have The Smarter Artist, which is Write, Publish, Repeat, the
book that we just published a month ago, Fiction Unboxed.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

Johnny Truant: 	Yay!
Jeff: 	I mean, your business model…this is going to break a few people's hearts that are
watching this, but your business model is right in that title. It's Write, Publish then the key piece
is Repeat, because no one is going to make a living, for the most part, unless you're some big
celebrity and you get some huge deal, in general, that first book, you're not going to make a
living off the first book. This is my opinion. I think it's your opinion, as well.
Sean Platt: 	I think it's almost fact. Because, at this point, you could look for…this is a big
premise in the book, is that's a lightning strike mentality. We really take a workmanlike
approach, that we go to our workshop each day, and we sand down the wood, and we slowly…
you can't be a carpenter and say, "Okay. I built a table on [inaudible]," when you build furniture
every day. That's what you do. That's how you spend your time in your workshop.
I mean, yeah, it's work, and we work hard, but it's fun. We like what we do, so it's not...
We don't feel like "Oh, man." We're not pouring concrete. Writing can be very hard, and it can
be emotionally exhausting, and there's a lot to do, but it's rewarding. We love what we do. Yes,
that is a big part of it.
Really, what it's about is…the repeat part that’s really important there is that you're
building a relationship with a reader. There's a lot of competition out there. This whole thing
with, there are no gatekeepers, like that's very true, but it's also totally not because the
gatekeepers, they just changed. They're not publishers anymore. Now, they're all those readers.
Those readers are going to decide one at a time whether you have a career or not.
If you please them, then they want to please you. They want to read your books, because
they don't care about you. Writers tend to be a little bit egocentric, and they think, "I just
finished my precious doily of a novel. The world can celebrate it," but the world is not going to
celebrate it, and the world doesn't care about you. If you touch a reader, if you make them feel,
or you make them think, or you make them remember, you give them some peace of nostalgia

that they are chewing on during dinner later, they are going to want more of that, because it's a
drug. We like to feel good. We like to think. We like to be entertained. For me, as much as I love
books, my true love is movies. I love a well told story. In the golden age of TV that we're in right
now, I love well scripted television.
Somebody like J.J. Abrams, he ticks all my boxes. As soon as I hear his name attached to
it, I'm interested. I want to know how is he going to tell this story. I think that it's very easy now
to do that with a reader, except it's also very hard, because there's so many people out there
doing it. Now, the good news is a lot of people are doing it terribly. They're doing it terribly, so
that does open…it gives a lot of opportunity to people who are smart and strategic. You have to
be able to tell a story. That's nonnegotiable.
Johnny Truant: 	All the competition goes away once you build a connection with an
individual reader. The example I gave in the book in that bookstore metaphor, Sean, was I was
walking around a Barnes & Noble, like a physical Barnes & Noble. Yes, there are still a few out
there. I was done with my current book, and I wanted to pick something up. I was like, "I don't
know. There's too many books here. There's too many options, like going to Amazon."
I scanned the titles, I scanned the shelves, and that doesn't do anything because I need to
know something in advance. It's rare that a cover would just catch me, and I would be like,
"Yeah, I'm going to pick that up." I found myself going to…I have my iPad, and I looked on like
Goodreads and did the lists, or whatever, and eventually, I just started looking to authors that I
already knew. I ended up picking up a Neil Gaiman book. I think it was Neverwhere at that
time. It was because Neil had already earned me as a fan.
Authors…you can't just go into your little cubicle and hide and say, "I'm going to be a
non-personality and just throw art out there, and the art's going to speak for itself." The art
doesn't speak for itself. You have to cast a wide net by having a lot of products, and you need to
have a platform. You need to be visible.
We get people all the time…this shouldn't be possible, but it is…who will say, "I read
Write, Publish, Repeat," or "I watched your Fiction Unboxed project," which was that for
writers. That was not for readers, where we wrote a book live, or "I listen to your podcast, and I
started reading Unicorn Western, or now I've read the whole Beam series." We get people that
come over from our nonfiction to our fiction, but it's because they like us, and we're sort of a
bookshelf of one for them. They're like, "Well, I know these guys, and I like them, so I'm going to
check their stuff out." The people that worry about this just deluge of books, it's like you only
have to worry about that if nobody knows who you are, and you're anonymous, which is where
we all start, but then you just have to build those relationships and keep them.

Sean Platt: 	The people listening to this right now, you already have secret cards in your deck,
because you already understand the value of email, and the value of that relationship, and the
value of a launch, and marketing, and all of that stuff. It is, it is a fact. If you have two writers,
and they're of equal ability, they could both tell a story, but one of them understands marketing,
and one of them doesn't, or thinks he's too good for it. "My stories are so special. My doily is so
fantastic that I don't have to tell people about it." The guy who can market is always going to
win.
I would even make the argument that the guy who can tell an okay story but really knows
how to connect with his reader, is going to do better than the guy who could tell a fantastic story
who does not have a list yet.
Jeff: 	100% agree with that. I think I had one of those doilies. I just…what were three months
or five months off publishing my first book, and I wrote…that thing took me at least three years,
probably longer. I sort of am scared of…I actually look back on my journals and…I sweated over
every word.
Sean Platt: 	We could've got you done in a month.
Jeff: 	I know you could have. It's what kills me. I think most authors are like that with their
first book, right? I mean you said, Johnny…
Johnny Truant: 	I was.
Jeff: 	I mean, it's just natural…it’s your first time you're coming out. First of all, there's that
whole perfectionism thing. I'd love to hear…because you guys have obviously sort of gotten past
the perfectionism piece from what I'm getting. Then, there's a lot of people that…
Sean Platt: 	Perfect is the enemy of done.
Jeff: 	Exactly. I like what Tony Robbins says, "Perfect or perfectionism is the lowest common
standard you could possibly have, because if you're going to hold off until you're perfect, you're
never going to put something out." It is no standard. You want to be extraordinary, or excellent,
or great, but perfect, it is a nonstarter. I'd love to hear your take on that. Then, so many people
that are going to be watching us, they're going to have their first book almost done, and now it is
going to be so depressing to hear that you're telling them it's going to five bucks. That one, it's
going to be five. Could you speak to that?
Johnny Truant: 	I know it depressed me when I realized that, because I just had the one
book. It was a behemoth, and it took me…I wasn't writing it for 12 years, but I wrote it and it

took a long time, and then I revised it like 16 times, and then I put it away, and then I revised it
some more, and I sent it off to traditional publishers. It is a bummer, but unfortunately no
matter how much of a bummer it is, it is a fact.
You can get around it a little bit by writing shorter books, although we would advise
strongly against doing anything that cuts corners. If you're like, "I'm just going to throw out a
bunch of little books specifically because I need to have a lot of books, and the books shouldn't
be this short, but screw it. I need to get them out." That's a bad idea. The worst thing you can do
is leave a reader unsatisfied. That said, the first series that I started with after that first
behemoth was called "Fat Vampire," which came out of a joke at Dave's expense on our Better
Off Undead podcast, much like some of our other stuff.
It was 30,000 words. The vampire story is pretty simple, like even when you add the
twist that I did, and so that was not hard to tell that complete story. People liked it enough, and I
liked it enough that I said, "Okay, well I want to see what happens next," and so those are short.
Sean and my first books together were Unicorn Western out of another joke at Dave's
expense. Those were 25,000 words each, and we wrote nine of them. Of course, that was kind of
a cheat because nobody considers the individual books to be individual books. It's really the big
thick behemoth that Sean is reaching for right now.
Sean Platt: 	Right. This is Unicorn Western.
Johnny Truant: 	That's really the book.
Sean Platt: 	Yeah, it's nine books. We put them out nine books, but they're not even available
individually now, are they? Johnny, didn't we…
Johnny Truant: 	Just the first one, which is great.
Sean Platt: 	Yeah. The thing is you want to take as long as you need to tell a story, but again,
it's a long-term thing. If you disappoint that reader because they read your book and I they
think, "This was good, but it really should have been longer."
Like, we're good storytellers. We understand story craft. We do it all day, every day. In
the Collective Inkwell [inaudible], which is the horror book, we had a book that came out earlier
this year called "Crash." It's a novella. It's not long at all. It was very personal to Dave. It's a very,
very good book, but it's short, and has 103 reviews as of today. Most of them are very, very
favorable, but the bad reviews that we have, they ding the length. They wanted more. They
wished it was longer.

Book Launch Bonus – INTERVIEW
Now, it wouldn't have really worked as longer book. It really was the story Dave wanted
to tell, Most of our stuff is really long. This wasn't short because we were trying to get a book to
market real quick. This was the length the story was supposed to be, but people still didn't like
that. You really do have to look at your story and think, "Am I serving the word count
appropriately?" If you're not, then you have to write more.
Now, I think this is a little bit different for nonfiction. I think this is a good time to kind if
draw the distinction between fiction and nonfiction, because with fiction, you do need multiple
titles, for sure, but you're also making your money off the titles themselves. With nonfiction,
nonfiction works remarkably well as lead-gen. You're probably not just making your money off
of that nonfiction book, it's a business card, right?
What's the difference between a speaker who gets $500 and a speaker who gets 10,000?
A book. That's it. You need that book because it's a calling card, it proves your authority. It's
absolutely worth getting out there even if you only have the one book, but that's a different
business. Now, if you do have the nonfiction book and it is only your one book, you're absolutely
wasting your time if you don't have a proper funnel, you don't have a place for your reader to go
when they're done with that book to get something else to get to get more. I read Launch. He
tells me where to go. He tells me to go not just at the end of the book, but all through the book.
"Hey, you want resources? Go here."
That's how you have to use your nonfiction book, because you're not going to have…you
can serialize nonfiction, but it's just not the same. People are more allegiant to fiction authors
than they are to nonfiction authors, because nonfiction, people are going for the answer to a
solution. They want to know how to do this one thing, and they want a problem solved. Fiction,
they want to be entertained, so they know…if you're a nonfiction author and you've solved 30
problems in 30 different books, it's unlikely that somebody has those exact 30 problems. If they
are, they need help, or more help than you can give them.
There are exceptions to that. Malcolm Gladwell…
Jeff: 	I was about to say…yeah. He tells great stories.
Sean Platt: 	He's a storyteller, right. Your people are allegiant not to the information, but to
the voice, which is what fiction authors have that nonfiction authors don't have it. I think there's
a good lesson there. Even if you're just writing that one nonfiction book, find ways to embed
story into it because that's what people remember. Nobody remembers dry facts. My son
remembers dry facts, but he's weird. Most people, they remember stories before they remember
facts, and so if you personalize it and you are able to be more intimate with your reader through
communicating stories which are more personal to you, then you're going to resonate, they're
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

going to care. When you have something to sell at the end of that book, then it's going to have

Jeff: 	I think of it like in terms of school. If you remember back to school, or a seminar, or a

to remember the story that your fifth grade teacher told, or the story that this speaker told.

Johnny Truant: 	Yes. I want to preface this because I just ... Sean said earlier that anybody
who's in this audience sort of has a leg up because you understand marketing, you understand

Sean Platt: 	The ratio was crazy good. I am never bothered by an unsubscribe, and you
shouldn't be either, because those people are not your customers. They are not your readers. If
somebody's complaining to you that you're emailing them, and they signed up to hear from you
when you have something to say, then you need to get them off your list, because all they're
doing is taking up space. You want to talk to people who want to hear from you. That's
marketing at its most elegant. You're just simply taking stuff and marrying it to the people who
most want it, and will most benefit from it.
Jeff: 	Yeah. I don't think it's probably different in the fiction, but I think of marketing as good

moments, but whatever. As we continued writing these, we did…the first one is free. The second

Now, after about the fourth or fifth book, we stopped talking about the saga because then

Sean Platt: 	Zero friction.
Johnny Truant: 	Zero friction. I use the example of Angry Birds for this, which is nonfiction

cake. We bought all the games. It all happened because I bought…I bought...I downloaded the
free one. If that first thing had been $.99. Like, it's a dollar, but I wasn't that interested. I was
interested to try it because it's free, but I'm not going to spend a buck. That's our philosophy, try
Unicorn Western 1 free. Try Beam Episode 1 free, and then upgrade to the season if you want,
that sort of thing. Then, you just keep building that vertical.

something like that for Unicorn Western 1. Our average for Unicorn Western full saga is 4.9 out

Johnny Truant: 	Yeah. Your loss leader functions as sort of a pool skimmer that scrapes all

Jeff: 	Right. What are the mechanics of…the first volume is free, is that free on Amazon? How
are you giving it away?
Sean Platt: 	When Johnny says free, he means permafree. Our first title is ideally permafree.
Now, there's a way to get free on Amazon, but we don't like it.
Johnny Truant: 	Temporarily free.
Sean Platt: 	Temporarily free, and that's KDP Select. If you're in KDP Select, you're basically
making a trade with Amazon, and you're saying, "I'm going to agree to be totally exclusive and
only give this away" or, I'm sorry…only put it on Amazon for distribution for a 90-days period.
In exchange, you get to be in Kindle Unlimited, which means people can borrow your book, you
also get five days that you can make the book free or make it $.99 and they'll put it on like a
countdown timer, you can run a sale.
In the meantime, you're not building any audience anywhere other than Amazon. You
can't even sell it on your site. You can't give it away on your site. You basically can't promote it.
Amazon has to do all of that for you. That bothers the entrepreneurial side of us a lot.
Now, as a fiction author, you could do really well on KDP Select. People will borrow, and
you can get a couple bucks per borrow. Because there's no friction, a lot of people will borrow.
It's a nice pool to be in, you just have to know what your strategy is. To us KDP Select is very
tactical, and we tend to think really strategically.
Johnny Truant: 	We only use it for very select stuff.
Sean Platt: 	Yeah, and always with a purpose, but permafree is where basically you put your
book on Amazon, and then you put it on Barnes & Noble and Smashwords, iTunes, pretty much
everywhere, and you try to get it free in as many places as you can. Then, Amazon will price
match it, because they'll say, "Well, we have to have the lowest price. If it's $0.00 on Kobo, we're
going to match that." Then, it becomes $0.00 on Amazon, and that's great.

Book Launch Bonus – INTERVIEW
Now, free is not the whole strategy. You have to make sure that on the last page of your
free book, it tells them where to go, or what to do, because that's the secret sauce there. You have
to know that, "Okay, this is going over to the next book in the series."
Now, another objection that we hear a lot is, "Okay. I've got my little series, but I don't
want to make the first book free. It's 100,000 words, and I spent two years writing it. I don't
want to make that free." That's a totally legitimate argument, especially if it takes you a couple of
years to write a book. Like, that's not doing you any good, and you could…
Jeff: 	It's like the typical person…I’m imagining a lot of people watching this, they've spent two
years writing that saga, and it's like, "I don't want to give that one away for free."
Sean Platt: 	Then, don't. We call this "Sideways stories." I feel like every book that you write…
now, this is great if you haven't written your book at all, because just make a note for yourself as
you get through that. Look for an opportunity to tell a sideways story. Every story has questions
that weren't answered, because life is like that. A million things happen to us each day. There's a
million decisions. Your life can go into any direction at any time.
Choose a vertices in the middle of your narrative that is…what would've happened here,
or what led up to this point, or what happened before this story, or what happened after this
story. After this story is probably a bad idea, actually, because you want them interested in the
larger picture. If you write some kind of a prequel or some kind of a story, that gets people
interested in your other story…that could be 10,000 words, and people can't complain about the
word count because it's free. What you really want to do is probably focus on your most
interesting character, on your most…whatever it is about your book that is really magnetic.
If you give it to 10 beta readers, and eight of them identify the same thing as "This is
what makes that book special," then that's what you want to gear your short towards, because
that is what's going to harmonize most. You absolutely can always think of something free. It
doesn't have to be your opus.
Jeff: 	That's a golden, golden tip. I love that. I mean, taking something sideways that leads into
your opus. That's beautiful. Love it. Obviously, that free thing, you want some type of a…if not
cliffhanger, certainly something that draws them to the next step, obviously. There's good ways
and bad ways to do that, I'm sure.
Sean Platt: 	Yeah, cliffhangers. We could talk a little bit about cliffhangers because
cliffhangers are juicy, juicy delicious, but if you do them wrong, you deserve a punch in the ear.
Jeff: 	I just saw Guardians of the Galaxy. Loved that movie.

Sean Platt: 	That was great.
Jeff: 	They've completed the story. They told a great story. Then, they set up the next movie,

Johnny Truant: 	We did this experiment. I have to give you a little bit of lead up before I
give you the cliffhanger story. We did a project called "Fiction Unboxed." We crowd-sourced it
on Kickstarter and we said, "Who wants to watch us write a book? Who wants to see every little
detail?" That's what the Fiction Unboxed book is about, basically. "Who wants to watch every
detail of us writing?"
We had a 30-day ticking clock to write a hundred thousand word novel. Not just write,
polish, publish, edit, whatever. Sean is looking for it, too. He doesn't have it. We're writing this,
and we're down to the wire. It's my last day of writing, and it's just like, "We're going to make it.
It's going to be tough to finish off the polish. I have to go through it a whole other time, but we're
going to make it. We're going to make it."
I'm done with my day's writing. I know I have one day left. No. I have a half a day left.
I'm going to come back and I'm going to finish it. I'm getting ready go to the gym in between,
and something occurs to me, and I text Sean, because we knew we wanted an aha at the end of
the book to open the world. Basically, without spoilers, a dream engine takes place in a closed
environment. We wanted to say well this discovery of like, "Look what's out there."
We knew we were leading up to that aha at the end. We had mapped it out. We were real
happy with what we had. I just needed to write this little codicil to say, "Here's what happened at
the end." I text Sean, and I'm just like, "I just have a few more hours. It will be awesome." I'm
like, "You know, it just occurs to me, we have a serial ending here. Do you think that's a
problem?"
Then, there was this pause, because I knew he was already engaged. I knew he was
watching the text, and so I did not want a pause at that point. Like I wanted, "No. It's cool. It's
awesome" right away. Then he goes, "Yeah, I think we got a problem."
In a serial…like, think of serialized TV, those can just end. They used to be like, "Okay,
you've reached the end of this episode of Weeds. Tune in next week." You go, "Ah, but
whatever." Even for a season, like Breaking Bad, like you're just going, going, going, going,
"There's the cliff."

For a series book, or a series movie like Guardians of the Galaxy, you have to close the
story arc pretty much entirely, and then you can open a new wound for the next season. You
have to have a major thing closed. We didn't have that. We had it like serial. We had, go, go,
they're going, they're going. They're getting through this thing, and then it stops. We're like,
"That's not good."
Sean Platt: 	Yeah, that's punching the reader in the face. You can't do that. You really have to
give them closure. Here's the good news, because I know there's a lot of nonfiction authors
listening to this, cliffhangers work for nonfiction, too, you just have to do them right, and you
have to treat your reader with respect, right?
Write, Publish, Repeat has a cliffhanger. It's a soft cliffhanger, but basically ... It was
totally unintentional until we knew that we weren't going to be able to finish the book the way
we thought, so we're like, "Cliffhanger time. Ding, ding, ding."
Johnny Truant: 	We'll write the next book.
Sean Platt: 	Right. Because it was mapped out as a 50,000 word book, and ended up going
120,000 words. We didn't touch the productivity section that we had talked about. We got to the
very end, and we said, "Okay, well we we're going to write this whole book, but we couldn't. If
you want the productivity stuff, we're going to do that next year. Go join our list."
That's a very effective list builder. Without that cliffhanger…if we had just said, "Hey, if
you like this and you want to hear more stuff like this, go to our list." That would not have been
nearly as effective as, "Hey, you want the next book," which it was done in kind of a cliffhanger.
It can really work really well.
Johnny Truant: 	I like that you stepped to the side without me actually finishing the dream
engine story. I'll keep it brief. The end of that story is that we then had to add an extra day, and
fix the whole ending. We had to create an enemy. We had to go back and say, "Okay, so we need
to manufacture. We need to see it through ... We have like five days left to do everything. Let's
add an extra day to rough draft, and we need to create something that closes that book." Then,
yes, you do want that cliffhanger at the end.
I'm glad you pointed out the cliffhanger because in a series book, you want people going,
"I do want to know what happens next," but not in a way where you're like, "But nothing got
finished. That sucks." It's a fine line.
Jeff: 	Yeah. We're like, "I paid for a book, and I got like 95% of a book, and they didn't finish
the thing for me. Now, they want to get more money from me."

because the actual Fiction Unboxed book is 60,000 words, and it's a narrative, it's a story. It's a,

The problem was, we couldn't launch it like Write, Publish, Repeat because the audience

pay for it," because we don't want to piss readers off. We want to be cool.
We decided, "Okay, we're just going to go ahead and give them this as an extra freebie
bonus," but the problem with that is that how you get traction on Amazon is that you get into hot
new releases, and you get into Ozobots. If we're just giving it away, then we don't get into any of
those lists. Even at a pre-launch price of $2.99, it would kind of suck to go to our list and say,
"Hey, you know that thing that we just gave you for free, would you be awesome and buy it again
for $2.99?" Yes, some of our people would do that, because they're that awesome, but most of
them wouldn't, and we'd be jerks for asking.
We had to come up with some kind of a cookie, like a really good cookie that made it

Jeff: 	Right. Cool. Well, thanks so much, guys. How about last words of wisdom, especially for
our fiction writers that have now learned all these marketing chops, and they want to rock the
world with their work?
Sean Platt: 	I would say just, you're not making a doily. That first draft, it really is about
speed. You can edit and revise and make it better later. My basic formula is, if let's say you're
going to go through this three times, you want to say it. Say what you mean. Then, say it well.
Those are different steps. The longer you take on that first draft, the more you are self-editing
and inhibiting your natural voice. The faster you can get it out, the more…the truer it's going to
be to who you are and what you really have to say. You can refine that later. Really, I firmly
believe that for a fiction story, the faster you get that first draft out, the better it's going to be in
revision later.

Johnny Truant: 	My advice, my words of wisdom, are…I’ve gotten some people saying this
is really demoralizing advice, and some people telling me that this is really heartening advice.
Okay. We'll just see how you take it. It's that if you're in fiction to make money, you better love
fiction for its own sake. There was a Kindle gold rush period, and it is over, and it never had any
likes to begin with. It is not a sustainable model. You will almost certainly not get rich quick, or
even a parody of quick in fiction. It is a long haul. It is very hard, and you have to really, really
work, and you still are going to have periods where it's tough.
Now that said, for me that's actually…like, I like that realism because this is just about
math at this point. Like self-publishing means you publish one book. Even if that book makes
you $5 a month…now, hopefully it does better than that, but even if it does, like…I think we all
know math well enough to know that if you add up enough fives, you get to a living, right?
It didn't used to be that way. It would be nothing. Your book is denied. Needless titles
can now be published because you do it. If you keep at it long enough, you will succeed, if you
produce enough stuff and you keep at it, but it is a long road.
Sean Platt: 	It's a beautiful articulation of the Long Tail. I think that a lot of writers, they look
at it - at publishing their book - as that's the finish line, right? Like, "I'm done. I'm done. I'm
done. I don't have to do anything more." It's a process, not an event. When you're done, that's
great. Go do another one. If the idea of that pains you, then it's probably not for you anyway,
because you're not truly loving the writing of the fiction. The repeat part shouldn't be painful, it
should be exciting.
Johnny Truant: 	Right.
